Today we're looking at the new mixture of experts paper from DeepMind. The core idea is that instead of routing tokens to every expert, the router learns a sparse activation pattern. They show that with 8 experts and top-2 routing, you get most of the quality of a dense model at one third the inference cost. The interesting part is the load-balancing loss, which prevents expert collapse — that's the failure mode where the router sends everything to one or two experts and ignores the rest. They use an auxiliary loss that encourages roughly equal token assignment across experts. On the benchmarks, the model matches a 70B dense model on MMLU while using only about 22B active parameters per forward pass. They also compare against switch transformer and show that top-2 is consistently better than top-1 for the same compute budget. The training was done on 1.4T tokens, and they note that MoE models seem to benefit more from data than dense ones, which has implications for the scaling laws community. Towards the end they discuss inference serving challenges, especially around batching when different requests activate different experts.
